It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. The solution to this is to provide, and justify, systematic protocols to facilitate sorting of what we refer to as “ambiguous” cases below. Second, different types of documents will need slightly different ways to disentangle the 4PTs. For example, our careful review of journal articles on sustainability policy and governance requires laborious coding of highly complex articles. Our original focused on the Journal of Regulation and Governance was used to both revise our 4PT distinctions, and then to go back, recode, and assess intercoder reliability. However, our approach to coding abstracts for course syllabi at Yale’s environment schools was much swifter and faster – as we only needed to see whether the course championed a utility outcome or not, and whether it was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. Both coding exercises permitted the development of dominant “key words” within each both deductively and inductively. This means that one solution for accelerating coding through quantitively analysis of text is to identify the prevalence of different key words associated with one problem type over the others. Of course this approach is not perfect because sometimes some concepts such as “cost effectiveness” analysis can be used to achieve any kind of Type 4, 3 2 and 1 outcomes, while “cost benefit” reinforces Type 2. It may be difficult for using a key word approach alone to therefore uncover these nuances. Hence, while this approach may not be as nuanced as the human coding orientation, it promises to provide more swift data that itself, will be useful. And as along as standardizes approach is taken, it will still produce useful data even if that set might be slightly different than if laborious human coders had applied our more nuanced approach. Finally, and to integrate these different efforts, our ultimate goal is to draw on AI techniques that might best replicate human coding, but in much less time. We make four contributions: First, we perform a comprehensive analysis of how regulators are using customer engagement, and offer a simple model for understanding different customer engagement initiatives. Second, we review assessments of customer engagement. We ﬁnd that there are no quantitative, empirically robust assessments of the effectiveness of customer engage- ment as a regulatory tool. Third, we develop two detailed case studies of an energy regulator and a water regulator that are in the forefront of customer engagement efforts. We ﬁnd that there is no direct link between the engagement strategy used and the economic incentives received by a ﬁrm. Finally, we propose a framework for improving the customer engagement process. The new framework relies on microeconomics, modern tools of program evaluation, and supplying the regulated ﬁrm with direct incentives to engage with the customer. Keywords: beneﬁt–cost analysis, experiment, law and economics, regulation. 1. Introduction Regulation is pervasive throughout the world. Government authorities frequently regulate the prices of water, gas, electricity, rail, mail, air travel, and telephone in various ways. Objectives for this regulation vary, but they include promoting operational and economic efﬁciency, protecting against monopoly, and addressing equity and environ- mental concerns. During the last decade, customer engagement (CE) has become a central part of modern utility regulation. The essence of CE is that regulated ﬁrms involve customers more directly in their business decisions, particularly when preparing for rate cases or price reviews.1 Utilities can engage with customers either directly or through negotiation with groups representing customers in regard to prices and other dimensions of service. The novelty of this approach lies in the direct interaction with customers, and that it may help to determine what customers want. This information can then be incorporated in the regulatory process to achieve customers’ preferred outcomes. Despite the increasing popularity of CE, there is not one widely accepted deﬁnition.2 In this paper, we deﬁne CE as a regulatory approach aimed at understanding customer preferences and determining how to best meet those preferences. In practice, this approach can take different forms. In some cases, customer groups directly engage and negotiate with regulated ﬁrms. In other cases, the formal analysis of customer demands by regulated ﬁrms is an important component of regulatory decisionmaking. This paper makes four contributions: First, we perform a comprehensive review of how regulators are using CE, and offer a simple model for understanding different CE initiatives. At one extreme, we deﬁne “Negotiation” Correspondence: Robert Hahn, Smith School of Enterprise and the Environment, University of Oxford, South Parks Road, Oxford OX1 3QY, UK. In this form of CE, the regulator plays a more passive role and may simply agree to implement outcomes of a successful negotiation. At the other extreme, we deﬁne “Customer-centric Regulation,” where regulated ﬁrms are required to engage with their customers. The regulator reviews these engagement efforts and may incorporate them in the outcome of the price review. The utility can receive rewards from the regulator for “good” CE. Second, we review assessments of CE in the literature. We ﬁnd that there are no quantitative, empirically robust assessments of the effectiveness of CE as a regulatory tool. We argue that part of the problem is methodo- logical: it is difﬁcult to specify a counterfactual from which to measure the effectiveness of CE efforts. Third, we develop two detailed case studies of an energy regulator and a water regulator in the United King- dom (UK) at the forefront of CE efforts: the Ofﬁce for Gas and Electricity Markets (Ofgem) and the Ofﬁce for Water Services Regulation Authority (Ofwat).3 In both cases, regulators and stakeholders are generally satisﬁed with the outcomes of CE. Regulated ﬁrms appear to have increased their engagement efforts after the regulators explicitly incorporated CE in their price reviews. We ﬁnd, however, that incentives for ﬁrms to engage with their customers, and to use particular tools in the process, are not clearly speciﬁed. There does not appear to be a direct link between the engagement strategy used and the economic incentives received by a ﬁrm. Fourth, we discuss the ways in which regulators could reﬁne the current regulatory approach and suggest a new framework for improving CE. The new framework applies to all existing forms of CE. It relies more heavily on microeconomics, modern tools of program evaluation, and adjusting the incentive structure for undertaking CE. We identify three ways in which this framework might improve the efﬁciency of CE as currently practiced: (i) by introducing economic efﬁciency explicitly as a yardstick for evaluating CE; (ii) by providing ﬁrms and cus- tomer groups with direct incentives for good CE; and (iii) by making greater use of ﬁeld experiments and other program evaluation techniques to provide more robust estimates of customer preferences. Section 2 reviews the theory and practice of CE in utility regulation and provides an assessment of the effec- tiveness of CE based on academic evaluations. Section 3 examines CE from the perspective of the regulated ﬁrm using the two case studies. Section 4 identiﬁes ways in which regulators might improve CE efforts by expanding elements of the framework. Section 5 concludes and suggests areas for future research. 2. Customer engagement (CE) in theory and practice This section provides an overview of the different approaches to CE used by regulators around the globe. We begin by outlining the origins of CE and the general role envisioned by the regulator. To help frame the discus- sion, we present four stylized models of CE. The models differ in the extent of regulator involvement in the engagement process. We then review how regulators interpret CE across a range of sectors and countries. This analysis reveals that the process of CE differs along several key dimensions for different regulators. We conclude by investigating the current state of the academic literature in evaluating the success of CE regulation. We ﬁnd a lack of credible evidence on the economic impacts of CE on customers. 2.1. The origins of CE To understand the origins of CE, we provide a brief overview of traditional models of regulation. Typically, regulators must balance the needs of customers with the needs of the ﬁrms that are regulated. This turns out to be a very difﬁcult challenge (Kahn 1970/1971; Joskow & Noll 1981). Regulators often have very limited information on the cost structure of the ﬁrms they regulate and the willingness of customers to pay for various services (Laffont & Tirole 1993; Joskow 2008). Furthermore, in many cases, the objectives of the regulator are not well speciﬁed in legislation. Often, for example, there are calls for the regulator to act in the “public interest,” but that term is not clearly deﬁned (Joskow 1974; Posner 1974). Economists generally agree that a market with a reasonable level of competition is preferable to a situation in which regulators regulate prices and/or quality of service (Stigler & Friedland 1962; Joskow & Noll 1981; Vickers & Yarrow 1988). Beyond that consensus, there is no widespread agreement on the best forms of regulation. Further- more, there are many different regulatory models that have been employed in different regulated sectors. While the details differ, there are two general regulatory models that are typically discussed in the literature. The ﬁrst is a model in which ﬁrms are allowed to achieve a reasonable rate of return on their investments while © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 2 R. Hahn, R. Metcalfe, and F. Rundhammer A new trend in utility regulation Page 3: expensing labor and energy costs. Prices are then set to recover the “costs of service” while allowing for a “fair” rate of return (Kahn 1970/1971; Joskow & Schmalensee 1986). This is generally referred to as “rate-of-return” regulation. The basic idea is to allow the ﬁrm to charge a price that would arise in a hypothetical competitive market based on its ﬁxed and variable costs. Under rate-of-return regulation, ﬁrms may make excessive investments in their capital stock relative to labor to achieve greater proﬁts, thus leading to inefﬁciencies in production. The classic treatment is provided in Averch and Johnson (1962) and the resulting inefﬁciency is sometimes referred to as the “A-J effect,” named after these authors (also see Stigler & Friedland 1962). Joskow (1974), in a seminal article, showed that the regulatory pro- cess is richer than that modeled by Averch and Johnson. Joskow found that regulators act much more passively in the real world than assumed by Averch and Johnson. Generally, price reviews are not a response to increasing rates of return but rather are “[…] triggered by ﬁrms attempting to raise the level of their rates or to make major changes in the structure of their rates” (Joskow 1974, p.12). If nominal prices stay constant or even decrease, the regulator remains largely passive despite the potential for rates of return to increase. As a consequence, regulators set prices only periodically and usually in response to rate applications by a ﬁrm (Joskow 1973). These reviews can be lengthy and require deep knowledge of cost structures (Joskow & Schmalensee 1986; Newbery 1998; Jos- kow 2008). Thus, regulation only slowly adjusts to changes in the cost structure. Between regulatory reviews, ﬁrms aim to maximize proﬁts (or other ﬁnancial measures) and have incentives to improve efﬁciency to achieve this goal (Joskow & Noll 1981; Joskow & Schmalensee 1986; Armstrong & Vickers 1991; Newbery 1998). Because of the large transaction costs and inefﬁciencies thought to be associated with rate-of-return regula- tion, economists searched for other models that might provide better incentives and regulatory outcomes. In 1983, Professor Stephen Littlechild suggested a model of “price-cap” regulation for the telecommunications sector in the UK.4 Many other regulators in the UK and elsewhere later adopted this model.5 The basic idea behind price-cap regulation is that the regulator sets a price limit on a service or group of services for a particular utility.6 The utility can only charge customers up to this price but can keep any savings associated with efﬁciency gains until the next price review commences (Littlechild 1983). In practice, the price is based on an index that is usually adjusted by some measure of efﬁciency gains, using the “RPI-X” formula.7 RPI represents a retail price index (or consumer price index outside of the UK) that captures inﬂation and X often represents the average expected efﬁciency savings of a regulated ﬁrm in the sector.8 In theory, X could be set to represent efﬁciency gains that could be realized in a competitive market (Bernstein & Sappington 1999).9 The main rationale for RPI-X was that it provides better incentives for ﬁrms to reduce their production costs (Cabral & Riordan 1989; Braeutigam & Panzar 1993; Vogelsang 2002). Because ﬁrms have ﬂexibility in terms of price setting, they can increase their proﬁt by producing more efﬁciently. There are also other beneﬁts of this reg- ulatory approach, such as efﬁciency and innovation, and promotion of competition (Greenstein et al. 1995; Little- child 2014a, p. 154). Evidence from the United States (US) generally supports these claims across different sectors (Mathios & Rogers 1989; Kaestner & Kahn 1990; Newbery 1998). Price-cap regulation is not without shortcomings, however. Substantial transaction costs are associated with the process, particularly the way in which initial prices and X are set (Newbery 1998; Joskow 2008, 2014; Littlechild 2012a). Because the regulator is uncertain about a ﬁrm’s true costs, prices may be set relatively high to ensure that high-cost ﬁrms can recover costs. In this case, ﬁrms may extract rent from customers by charging above their true cost (Newbery 1998; Joskow 2008). There may also be incentives to reduce the quality of a product to further increase proﬁts (Kahn 1970/1971). In practice, rate-of-return and price-cap regulation models face similar challenges. Both regulatory approaches rely on extensive knowledge of a ﬁrm’s cost structure, customer demands, and other important measures in the sector (Newbery 1998; Joskow 2008). Because of a constantly changing environment, both approaches also require periodic review (Joskow 1974; Newbery 1998). In the case of price-cap regulation, for example, Littlechild (2012a) argues that such reviews gradually had to consider a wider range of elements, including potential efﬁ- ciency improvements, investment programs, and proposals for innovation (Krieger 1995). Furthermore, in many cases, these regulatory processes were not immune from politics (Peltzman 1976; McChesney 1987; Noll 1989).10 The practical implementation of the two forms of regulation may not differ substantially in many cases (Newbery 1998; Joskow 2008). To set the initial price in price-cap regulation, for example, regulators must con- sider a reasonable rate of return for the regulated sector. Therefore, price-cap regulation uses principles of rate- © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 3 A new trend in utility regulation R. Hahn, R. Metcalfe, and F. Rundhammer Page 4: of-return regulation when determining important regulatory variables at the time of a price review. The literature also discusses other regulatory frameworks that more explicitly combine elements of rate-of-return and price-cap regulation (Laffont & Tirole 1993). Problems with existing approaches led scholars and practitioners to search for improvements to the regula- tory framework. One recent development in the regulatory landscape is a paradigm shift from regulator-focused toward more customer-focused regulation (Littlechild 2014a; Bush & Earwaker 2015; Heims & Lodge 2016b). The novelty of this paradigm is not the consideration of customer needs per se – a goal that has been important in regulation for decades – but rather the direct engagement with customers. If successful, this process can help resolve some of the information asymmetries involving regulators, ﬁrms, and end customers. The integration of customer opinion in the regulatory process is not a new phenomenon. In the US, for example, various stakeholders have long had the opportunity to present their views on proposed changes to the regulator. A typical rate case is opened after a utility ﬁles a case to change rates. Stakeholders, such as consumer groups or environmental agencies, then prepare and ﬁle testimony with the regulator. After case ﬁlings have been received, the rate case is formally processed during a hearing before the regulator, with cross-examination of wit- nesses from all intervenors. After the hearing, the regulator makes its decision and the rate is implemented (Joskow 1973, 1974, 2008). Alternatively, the regulator may facilitate negotiations between the utility and stake- holders that lead to a settlement outside of formal hearings (Littlechild 2007, 2009).11 As we discuss further, the importance of settlements appears to have increased in recent years. In the UK, a greater role for customers has been more explicitly incorporated in regulatory proceedings. Regulators typically ask for direct engagement between utilities and their customers prior to a price review. Throughout the development of a business plan, cus- tomer input is collected that can aid the regulator and regulated ﬁrms in deﬁning outcomes and determining prices that reﬂect customer demand for the regulated goods and services. We restrict the subsequent analysis to cases where CE is a formal part of the regulatory process or where the regulator allows for direct negotiation between customers and regulated ﬁrms.12 We interpret CE as a regulatory approach aimed at understanding customer preferences and determining how to best meet those preferences. The goal from this perspective is to increase the beneﬁts that the ﬁrm provides to customers, or overall beneﬁts pro- vided to both the regulated ﬁrm and the customer. 2.2. Stylized models of CE: Power to the people Figure 1 provides a stylized way of thinking about different forms of CE that helps to formalize some of the contribu- tions to this literature.13 In this formulation, the regulator has two choices. The ﬁrst is whether to require a formal assessment of the impacts of changes to prices or the terms of service on customers, or using analytical tools to esti- mate customer demands.14 These demands are typically for a private good, such as energy or water, but could also be estimated for a local or global public good, such as environmental amenities. The second choice is whether the regula- tor allows (or even encourages) the ﬁrm and its customers to negotiate a deal on prices and terms of service directly. There are four cells in Figure 1 associated with the two different choices. The cells labeled “Negotiation” (top right) and “Customer-centric Regulation” (bottom left) represent the extreme cases for CE. Under “Negotiation,” Figure 1 Stylized models of customer engagement compared to traditional regulation. © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 4 R. Hahn, R. Metcalfe, and F. Rundhammer A new trend in utility regulation Page 5: the regulated ﬁrm directly negotiates a deal with customer representatives and other stakeholders, such as envi- ronmental groups or worker unions. The regulator will perform a traditional price review only if no deal is reached, or if the deal violates fundamental expectations of the regulator.15 This approach to regulation does not require the ﬁrm to use formal assessments of impacts on customers or customer demands. The polar opposite within this framework is the cell labeled “Customer-centric Regulation.” With Customer-centric Regulation, the regulator determines the prices and terms of service after the ﬁrm engages with customers, and presents its ﬁnd- ings formally to the regulator as part of its business plan or rate case testimony. There are two other cells in Figure 1. One corresponds to “Traditional Regulation” (top left), where the regulator sets the price and terms of service, but does not require the ﬁrm to formally assess customer demand or program impacts. This cell can be thought of as the status quo and includes both traditional rate-of-return and price-cap regulation. The other com- bines Negotiation with a requirement for formal assessment. We label this “Negotiation Plus” (bottom right). This taxonomy suggests an interesting tension between different perspectives on the role of regulation. On the one hand, Traditional Regulation and Customer-centric Regulation rely on a strong regulator that retains decisionmaking power. For example, with Customer-centric Regulation, the regulator remains a very active player in this “game” (Owen & Braeutigam 1978; Noll & Owen 1983). While the ﬁrm places greater focus on the cus- tomer to elicit customer preferences, the regulator still decides on the ﬁnal prices and terms of service. This ﬁnal decision may or may not incorporate the insights from CE.16 On the other hand, Negotiation and Negotiation Plus imply a more passive role for the regulator (Doucet & Littlechild 2006; Pollitt 2008). Successful negotiation may determine prices and terms of service without any inﬂuence from the regulator (Pollitt 2008). The goal of the regulator in this case may be to encourage and facilitate negotiation, and to create an environment conducive to fruitful negotiations. Proponents of limited regulatory inﬂuence likely prefer this approach to regulation.17 Another related concern is how to ﬁnance different regulatory approaches (Waddams 2014). Formal CE likely imposes additional costs on regulated ﬁrms, which may be passed on to the end customer. There may be several motives for incorporating CE in regulatory practice. Firms and customers may have bet- ter information about the cost structure and customer preferences than a central regulator. In this case, customers and ﬁrms may be better equipped to reach a mutually beneﬁcial agreement and overcome information asymme- tries (Littlechild 2012a, 2014b).18,19 Another motivation may be to reduce the impact of the regulator on eco- nomic outcomes. This may itself be seen as a desirable goal. CE may also create valuable insights that can inform current and future regulatory decisions. 2.3. CE in practice: A new trend These models are stylized. In practice, regulators select from a broad menu of options that involve CE. Table 1 introduces eight examples of regulators using some form of CE. As can be seen in column 4, the use of Customer-centric Regulation is a relatively recent development and is generally based on the British experience. Rundhammer Page 6: Negotiation is a much older element of regulation that is generally applied in the US.20 For example, the Florida Public Service Commission is a widely-studied example of retail regulation in the US.21 The history of CE illustrates how the relationship between regulators, regulated ﬁrms, and customers has evolved. In the US, negotiations between ﬁrms and customers gained in popularity as a mechanism to limit the role of a historically very strong regulator (Wang 2004; Doucet & Littlechild 2006; Littlechild 2007, 2009, 2012b). This movement is reﬂected by a shift from the top left to the top right cell in Figure 1. If negotiation between ﬁrms and customers is successful, the regulator does not have to play an active role in the regulatory process. In this case, CE offers the promise of reducing or completely removing the inﬂuence of the regulator (Pollitt 2008; Heims & Lodge 2016b). Decades later, Australia, Canada, and the UK also started to reconsider the inﬂuence of the regulator. As a result, the Civil Aviation Authority (CAA) ﬁrst explored a regulatory model based on negotiation between regu- lated ﬁrms and customers in the early 2000s (Littlechild & Mountain 2015). Unlike the US experience, UK regu- lators were not willing to give up their decisionmaking power. One way for these regulators to remain in control of the regulatory process was to formalize expectations and requirements for CE. This more formal approach to CE represents a shift from the top row to the bottom row in Figure 1. Within the bottom row, different regula- tors take several approaches to CE. For example, as we will describe in detail, Ofgem and Ofwat ﬁrmly positioned themselves in the Customer-centric Regulation cell. In contrast, the CAA and the Water Industry Commission for Scotland (WICS) allow for negotiations between regulated ﬁrms and customers. However, the airport regulator remained in charge of important elements of regulation and prescribed how agreements must consider customer preferences (Littlechild & Mountain 2015). Similarly, the negotiations in the Scottish water sector were based on numerous regulatory proceedings describing the regulator’s expectations. Many of these proceedings were released throughout the negotiation process to inform the negotiating parties about changes in the expectations of the regulator (Littlechild 2014b). Negotiations also built on insights from a customer forum that was required to develop a research program reﬂecting the views of customers (Littlechild & Mountain 2015). These regulatory approaches are best described as Negotiation Plus.22 The nature of CE is not the only difference across these examples. The regulatory landscape also differs in terms of the size of the sector, the number of ﬁrms and customers, the legal environment, and the information available to the regulator. Within a regulated sector, ﬁrms may differ substantially along important dimensions such as their cost structure, the types of customers they serve, and their economies of scale. Another major differ- ence is the structure of the periodic price reviews by the regulator. Regulators in the UK typically set prices for all ﬁrms simultaneously, while US regulators process reviews on a case-by-case basis. Different ﬁrms may also be regulated by different regulators. For example, in the US, state regulatory commissions typically regulate investor- owned utilities. Publicly owned utilities are regulated by other regulatory entities, such as municipal governments or federal regulators. Regulators also have different legal relationships with other regulatory agencies and may only be responsible for speciﬁc dimensions of a good or service. In the UK water sector, for example, Ofwat inter- acts with the Drinking Water Inspectorate, the Environment Agency, and several other bodies.23 Regulated sectors also differ in terms of the relationship between ﬁrms and their customers. For example, water utilities in the UK deliver services directly to households. In airport regulation, the customer is the airline rather than the passenger. In the latter case, both the airport and the airline are likely better informed and have stronger incentives to negotiate a mutually beneﬁcial result than the utility and the household in the former case. Residential households may also exhibit a wider range of preferences than a small number of proﬁt-maximizing ﬁrms. If asymmetric information is a considerable obstacle in a speciﬁc sector, or if there are many diverse parties on one side of the negotiation, regulators may be better off requiring formal CE channels (Waddams 2014). Similar arguments apply to regulation that focuses on households and commercial customers within a sector. Regulators may also differ in terms of their objectives. There may be goals of regulation beyond welfare, including process concerns (Joskow & Rose 1989). For example, successful Negotiation minimizes the formal reg- ulatory process. In contrast, Customer-centric Regulation introduces new elements into the regulatory process. These elements may increase the regulatory burden and the inﬂuence of the regulator. Regulators may also be interested in improving the legitimacy of regulation. Because customers provide input in the regulatory process under CE, outcomes may be seen as more legitimate (Ofwat 2013; Heims & Lodge 2016b). Finally, regulators © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 6 R. Hahn, R. Metcalfe, and F. Rundhammer A new trend in utility regulation Page 7: may want to use regulatory tools to achieve favorable distributional outcomes. Recent regulatory proceedings of regulators using CE reﬂect such concerns. For example, both Ofgem and Ofwat explicitly require engagement with vulnerable (e.g. low-income) consumers to elicit their needs (Ofgem 2013a; Ofwat 2013). Based on these examples, we offer four observations about the use of CE. First, regulators are involved in the CE process to varying degrees. Regulators using Negotiation or Negotiation Plus take on more passive roles in the process. These efforts have become more important in recent years in the UK and in the US where negotiated settlements have increased substantially as a fraction of overall rate cases (Krieger 1995; Doucet & Littlechild 2006; Littlechild 2012a). In contrast, regulators using the Customer-centric Regulation model tend to remain more hands-on. Even if customer representatives fully agree with the proposed business plan, the regulator may implement substantial changes based on its own assessment of the case. Second, different regulators deﬁne different roles for customer representatives. For example, Ofwat asks regu- lated ﬁrms to set up independent customer challenge groups (CCGs) that have particular roles (Ofwat 2013). The main role is to provide a customer perspective in the development of the business plan, and to challenge the ﬁrm to improve the quality of its engagement with customers (Ofwat 2013). The CCGs report to the regulator and to customers, not to the ﬁrm. Similarly, the WICS established a Customer Forum in cooperation with its sole regu- lated ﬁrm and Scotland’s national consumer council (Water Industry Commission for Scotland [WICS] 2014). The role of this Forum differed from a CCG and included direct negotiations with Scottish Water. In contrast, Ofgem did not prescribe company-level consumer panels. Rather, the regulator created a sector-wide consumer panel that mainly acted in an advisory role during the review of business plans (Ofgem 2013a). In the case of Florida, the state set up the Ofﬁce of Public Counsel, which represents citizens in several utility matters (Littlechild 2007). In addition to this state-level consumer counsel, other consumer groups and stakeholders can ﬁle testimony during rate cases. Third, it appears that regulators evaluate the success of CE differently. Regulators following the Negotiation or Negotiation Plus approach generally deﬁne success as an acceptable agreement between the utility and its cus- tomers (and other stakeholders). For Customer-centric Regulation, there is not typically a clear deﬁnition of what is a successful CE process. Furthermore, assessments of the effectiveness of engagement efforts vary. In our sam- ple of regulators, assessment mechanisms range from a letter grade system (Ofwat) to elaborate scorecards (Ontario Energy Board) to a trafﬁc light scale (Ofgem). In addition to the different ways to assign grades to a ﬁrm’s CE activities, the mechanisms also differ in terms of the assessment criteria used. For example, Ofgem only uses one criterion, which assesses the overall impact of CE on the proposed content of the business plan. In con- trast, Ofwat deﬁnes three criteria with broader questions aimed at different elements of the CE process (see Table 2). Finally, there are important differences in the incentives provided by regulators for effective CE. The clearest contrast is between the Negotiation model and the Customer-centric Regulation model. In the former, the main incentive for successful negotiation is the possibility of almost entirely circumventing the regulator’s inﬂuence, and achieving mutually beneﬁcial outcomes (Pollitt 2008). In the latter, regulators provide ﬁnancial and proce- dural incentives for the overall business plan proposal – of which CE is one element – based on their respective assessment mechanism. In recent years, there has been an important development toward the increased use of some elements of CE. These elements are not always part of a formal price review or rate case, and they may be carried out independently of regulatory proceedings. Many utilities in the US engage with customers on a variety of issues. Popular examples include energy efﬁciency programs, such as subsidies for the take-up of energy-efﬁcient tech- nology and the provision of detailed feedback to help customers make more informed energy or water demand choices (Geller et al. 2006). Another widely used policy lever is the introduction of new pricing schemes, such as time-of-use or fully dynamic pricing (Borenstein 2005; Faruqui & Sergici 2010). Many of these programs are designed to meet new challenges in the sector. In some cases, regulators require utilities to implement new pro- grams. For example, multiple states mandate Energy Efﬁciency Resource Standards that introduce quantitative long-term energy savings targets for utilities.24 In other cases, utilities may implement programs to provide credible evidence used in their rate case submissions or as a result of other private incentives, such as customer satisfaction.25 © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 7 A new trend in utility regulation R. Hahn, R. Metcalfe, and F. Rundhammer Page 8: 2.4. Academic assessments of CE The literature that assesses the effectiveness of CE as a regulatory tool is relatively recent. There are two general problems with the literature. First, effectiveness is not always clearly deﬁned. Second, even when effectiveness is clearly deﬁned, it is often difﬁcult to determine what would have happened in the absence of CE. A review of existing work in this area provides four insights. First, academics document that regulators and stakeholders have favorable beliefs and views on the role of CE for improving outcomes after implementation. This holds true across all of the case studies that are summarized in Table 1. For instance, Littlechild “was pleasantly surprised and impressed by the enthusiasm and innovation […] brought to the processes” by ﬁrms under the regulation of Ofgem and Ofwat (2014a, p. 157). Similarly, the experiences of stakeholders during price reviews by the CAA and the WICS are generally positive (Pollitt 2008; Littlechild 2012a, 2014a,b; Heims & Lodge 2016b; Hendry 2016). Second, not all aspects of the ﬁrst generation of Customer-centric Regulation went smoothly. For example, in the CAA’s most recent reviews, some airports failed to reach an agreement with airlines (Pollitt 2008; Littlechild 2012a). These reviews, however, took place at a time of complex changes in the sector. Two of the three regulated airports wanted to be deregulated. Consequently, it might not be surprising that some negotiation was unsuccess- ful. For the water sector, Heims and Lodge (2016b) document a lack of guidance and repeated changes to the framework by the regulator that led to frustration during Ofwat’s price review in 2014. They suggest that some companies perceived that “a lot of time and effort had been spent ‘for nothing’” (Heims & Lodge 2016b, p. 14). Third, despite its important role in US regulation, Negotiation has not been widely discussed in economics lit- erature. Most work focuses on “negotiated settlements” in Florida (Doucet & Littlechild 2006; Littlechild 2007, 2009) and on the use of Negotiation by federal regulators (Wang 2004; Littlechild 2012b).26 The consensus in the literature is that Negotiation leads to better outcomes than traditional regulation from the perspective of the ﬁrm and the customer (Klein & Sweeney 1999). For example, Littlechild (2012a) ﬁnds that settlements are less time- consuming, less costly, and less uncertain than litigation for the regulated ﬁrm. In a later paper, Littlechild (2014a) highlights the potential of settlements to fundamentally change the regulatory framework. Florida pro- vides one example in which regulation moved from standard rate-of-return approaches to a system more closely resembling price-cap regulation. In another set of studies, Littlechild (2007, 2009) provides evidence that cus- tomers receive sizable additional beneﬁts from rate reductions that are primarily the result of negotiated Table 2 Assessment of customer engagement Regulator Grading scale Number of total categories Assessment category containing CE Assessment criteria Ofgem Trafﬁc light system 5 Process (i) Has the ﬁrm engaged with stakeholders, and explained how this has inﬂuenced its business plan? (ii) [...] demonstrated effective engagement with wider consumer interest, including environmental interests, generally and through their CCG? (iii) [...] demonstrated a robust approach to gathering WTP information and mapping this to its outcomes, performance commitments, and outcome delivery incentives? Assessment of a ﬁrm’s customer engagement (CE) process for the two case studies. This information only considers the cate- gory directly related to CE. Regulators evaluate business plans in many other categories. Column 4 provides the name of the category including CE. In the case of Ofgem, the “Process” category also includes assessment criteria unrelated to CE. Ofgem assigns one grade on the trafﬁc light system (green, yellow, red) to the overall category; Ofwat assigns a letter grade (A–D) to each assessment criterion and then aggregates these individual grades to determine an overall grade for the assessment cate- gory.
