More than three quarters of rate reductions are achieved by settlements rather than litigation, with a mean value of reduction eight times larger than those realized by litigation. These conclusions rely on the implicit assumption that the only difference between ﬁrms’ proposals is whether they were resolved by Negotiation or standard rate case hearings; many other important factors may differ as well. Fourth, fundamental concepts of economic efﬁciency enter the discussion only tangentially. Evidence is mostly based on anecdotes or a comparison of price controls under voluntary CE with those achieved by tradi- tional regulation. To the best of our knowledge, no research seeks to theoretically or empirically derive consumer surplus or economic efﬁciency gains associated with the introduction of CE. This lack of clean academic evidence on the efﬁciency implications of CE may appear to be surprising. How- ever, we believe that the complex structure of the regulatory process paired with stark differences across regula- tory environments can explain this gap in the literature. A major challenge in evaluating CE regulation is the missing counterfactual (Joskow & Rose 1989). It is not immediately clear what the alternative regulatory approach would be, or how to construct a meaningful quantitative comparison of the beneﬁts and costs. Unfortunately, in all cases, a small number of ﬁrms coupled with multiple changes in the regulatory framework make it difﬁcult to assess the impact of CE on regulated ﬁrms or their customers. Another challenge is that consumer preferences are complex and multi-dimensional (Waddams 2014). Regu- lation that aims to reﬂect these preferences likely needs to consider outcomes beyond residential rates. Customer interests may be much more complicated than a preference for lower prices, such as concerns about the environ- ment and the quality of service. It is not clear how these different outcomes could be assessed and weighted with- out carefully formulating a framework that includes economics. A ﬁrst step for robust assessments is the deﬁnition of the goal of regulation, a question we will discuss in more detail in Section 4. While a precise answer to the question of whether CE improves the outcomes of regulation remains elusive, there is a second layer that is better suited for economic analysis. The shift toward assessing customer preferences will involve a range of new programs implemented by ﬁrms. Some of these programs may be ideally suited for modern evaluation techniques. Economists have access to a wide range of empirical tools developed to evaluate such interventions, including measuring the impacts on customers (Angrist & Pischke 2009). Learning how par- ticular programs affect demand, customer satisfaction, or measures of consumer surplus can inform both the ﬁrm’s and the regulator’s future decisions. Studies can be designed to elicit trade-offs among various outcomes, such as lower residential rates and environmental quality (Rosen 1974). 3. The CE process: Firm incentives and responses This section shifts the focus from the perspective of the regulator to that of the regulated ﬁrm. We examine the regulatory environment and ﬁrms’ CE efforts for two important cases in the UK: Ofgem and Ofwat. We use the case studies to highlight important elements of CE currently considered by regulators. The UK environment is particularly well suited for this inquiry because of its regulatory structure. Both of the regulators we study regulate multiple ﬁrms, which allows us to compare efforts across ﬁrms within a given regulatory structure and price review. For each ﬁrm, we have access to a detailed CE strategy that was submitted to the regulator as part of the ﬁrm’s business plan. We also observe the regulators’ assessments of engagement strategies undertaken by ﬁrms. These data enable us to establish a relationship between the ﬁrms’ efforts and the incentives received by the ﬁrms for their CE. Finally, the regulatory innovations by Ofgem and Ofwat have spurred similar efforts in other sectors in the UK and abroad.27 For example, Australia’s Essential Services Commission recently redeﬁned their regula- tory approach based on insights from the UK (Littlechild & Mountain 2015; Essential Services Commission 2016; Morton & van Bueren 2017). We begin by highlighting the prominent role of CE in regulatory proceedings. We then compare detailed data on CE efforts by regulated ﬁrms with the regulator’s evaluation of the ﬁrm’s business plans. Our analysis suggests there is not a direct link between the evaluation of a ﬁrm’s engagement process and the overall assessment of the business plan; CE is only one of many elements used to assess a business plan. Because the rewards that a ﬁrm receives depend on the overall assessment, there is only an indirect link between rewards and engagement efforts. We ﬁnd that ﬁrms responded to the call for CE and engaged in many different ways. Our conclusion highlights © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 9 A new trend in utility regulation R. Hahn, R. Metcalfe, and F. Rundhammer Page 10: the success of early CE efforts as perceived by the regulator but also discusses clear drawbacks of the current sys- tem from the standpoint of incentivizing ﬁrms to engage effectively. To develop a better understanding of how the CE process works in practice, we draw from price reviews con- ducted by Ofgem and Ofwat.28 These case studies allow us to gain detailed insights into ﬁrm responses to the call for CE because we have access to the business plans submitted by ﬁrms. We highlight the importance of CE in the regulatory proceedings of Ofgem and Ofwat used for determining the price controls for 2015–2023 (RIIO- ED1) and 2015–2020 (PR14), respectively.29 In the case of Ofwat, the regulator established a “three-tiered approach that will enable customers to engage with and inﬂuence all parts of their companies’ business plans” (2011, p. 2). The three tiers are: (i) direct local engagement of ﬁrms with their customer body; (ii) ﬁrm-level CCGs that include stakeholders from various organizations;30 and (iii) a sector-wide customer advisory panel. Ofgem speciﬁed a more limited role for CE and called for “enhanced engagement” that “will require companies to engage more effectively with their customers […]” (2010a, p. 17). Ofgem also requested that ﬁrms engage with customers during all phases of business plan development, beginning with early drafts and extending beyond the conclusion of the price review. Both regulators also developed a reward structure for ﬁrms that is partly based on the quality of their CE. Despite requiring extensive CE, neither Ofwat nor Ofgem mandate that the ﬁrms use speciﬁc analytical tools or engagement channels. The main argument for this light-handed approach is that ﬁrms are better equipped to devise effective engagement strategies because of their knowledge of their customer body. Ofgem’s Handbook for Implementing the RIIO Model states: “companies should take decisions about how best to understand and respond to the needs of their customers; and companies may wish to explore a range of techniques […]” (2010b, p. 16). At the same time, customer engagement was not to be a “box-ticking exercise,” further alluding to the ﬂex- ibility provided to ﬁrms (Ofgem 2012, p. 16; Bush & Earwaker 2015). The one exception is that Ofgem explicitly expects ﬁrms to consider “fuel-poor or vulnerable” customers and future consumers in their engagement efforts (Ofgem 2013a). Similarly, Ofwat “will not prescribe how they [ﬁrms] do this [engagement], but will provide high-level guid- ance and expect the companies to use good practice” (2011, p. 2). Part of this guidance is the expectation that “each company will use a wide range of information from its customers (e.g. from any complaints they make) and where necessary carry out any robust new customer research needed to understand its customers’ priorities for services and views on bills.” (Ofwat 2012, p. 2). The ﬁnal methodology for the price review in 2014 explicitly considers customers’ willingness to pay (WTP) as an important building block of robust research to value the ﬁrms’ goods and services (Ofwat 2013, p. 77). Ofwat (2013) also expects ﬁrms to engage with vulnerable and future consumers. After engaging with customers, each regulated ﬁrm submits a business plan to the regulator. This business plan proposes outcomes that ﬁrms aim to achieve during the regulated period. CE is intended to shape these out- comes and align them with customers’ needs (Ofgem 2013a; Ofwat 2013). For example, for the price review in 2014, Ofwat speciﬁed that “customer engagement is an important part of our [Ofwat’s] assessment of business plan quality - and we expect plans to demonstrate they reﬂect customers’ views” (2013, p. 59). Ofgem and Ofwat formally review these plans based on an evaluation process, summarized in Table 2. Row 2 presents details about Ofwat’s review, which consists of 13 “tests” that focus on different elements of the business plan. CE is the subject of only one of these tests. Each test contains several smaller evaluation criteria formulated as questions. Ofwat assigns grades on a letter scale (A–D) to all criteria and then aggregates these grades into an overall grade for each test.31 Row 3 summarizes a comparable process used by Ofgem. The basis of the assessment is a set of ﬁve core cri- teria. Each criterion consists of several related evaluation questions similar to Ofwat’s approach. CE is only fea- tured in one question on the “Process” criterion. Ofgem assigns each core criterion a grade on a trafﬁc light scale based on performance on the underlying evaluation questions. In the case of both regulators, the overall assessment of the business plan determines several key outcomes for the regulated ﬁrm. Firms do not receive an overall grade for their proposal. Instead, business plans may be con- sidered “high-quality” in the case of Ofwat (2013) or “well-justiﬁed” in the case of Ofgem (2013a).32 We use “high-quality” to refer to such business plans for both regulators. A ﬁrm gains access to several positive incentives if its business plan is perceived as high quality.33 Firms with business plans that are not considered high quality © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 10 R. Hahn, R. Metcalfe, and F. Rundhammer A new trend in utility regulation Page 11: do not gain access. Because CE is only one of the elements considered during the overall assessment, it does not directly affect important ﬁnancial and non-ﬁnancial outcomes for a ﬁrm.34 A high grade on the criteria related to CE does not guarantee a ﬁrm’s access to incentives. Incentives are similar across the two regulators. The ﬁrst incentive relates to shortening the duration of the price review process. Firms with a high-quality business plan receive an early determination of the price review. The difference between a regular determination and an early determination is about nine months for both regula- tors (Ofgem 2013a, Ofwat 2014a). A shorter review period allows the ﬁrm to allocate resources to other ventures and provides a longer planning horizon for the actual implementation.35 The second incentive is targeted at repu- tational beneﬁts. Both regulators publicize the outcomes of their assessments and speciﬁcally highlight high- quality business plans. This practice may positively affect customer and shareholder views of a ﬁrm. We are aware of some ﬁrms actively disputing their overall assessment, which leads us to believe that the high-quality designation matters to ﬁrms.36 A third type of incentive provides ﬁnancial beneﬁts to ﬁrms with high-quality business plans. These ﬁrms receive an up-front lump sum payment and a more favorable sharing rule.
